Snipara's tech stack:

- Frontend: Next.js 14 (App Router)  
- Monorepo / package management: Turborepo with pnpm workspaces  
- Backend / ORM: PostgreSQL (Neon) with Prisma ORM  
- Authentication: NextAuth.js (GitHub, Google, Email)  
- UI: Tailwind CSS + DaisyUI + Tremor  
- Billing: Stripe  
- MCP Server: Python 3.10+ with FastAPI  
- Safe code execution / runtime: RLM-Runtime (Docker-based)  
- Deployment: Railway (web app + MCP server)

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service — i.e., deliver only the most relevant, token‑efficient context so clients can get high‑quality LLM results without vendor lock‑in.

Key points:
- ~90% context reduction (e.g., compress 500K tokens → ~5K tokens of relevant content)
- Clients use their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in)
- High margins (pure compute; no LLM costs passed through)
- LLM‑agnostic (works with any MCP‑compatible client)

Business model: charge for context optimization queries (not LLM inference).

Short answer — Snipara exposes a set of MCP "rlm_*" tools grouped by purpose. Key tools (as shown in the docs):

- Primary (search / quick queries)
  - rlm_context_query — full semantic search + token-budgeted context
  - rlm_ask — quick, simple query (~2.5K tokens)
  - rlm_search — regex / keyword pattern search

- Power-user (planning / decomposition / batch)
  - rlm_plan — generate execution plans for complex tasks
  - rlm_decompose — break complex queries into sub-queries / chunks
  - rlm_multi_query — run multiple queries in parallel

- Team / multi-project / shared context
  - rlm_multi_project_query — search across all team projects
  - rlm_shared_context — fetch team/shared coding standards and docs
  - rlm_list_collections — list shared context collections
  - rlm_upload_shared_document — upload docs into a shared collection

- Templates / prompts
  - rlm_list_templates
  - rlm_get_template

- Document / context management
  - rlm_upload_document — upload/index a document
  - rlm_store_summary — save LLM-generated summaries
  - rlm_inject / rlm_context / rlm_clear_context — session/context injection & management
  - rlm_recall / rlm_remember — session memory (recall/remember)

- Utility / diagnostics
  - rlm_stats — usage / query stats
  - rlm_sections — browse documentation structure / sections

(There are ~43 MCP tools in total; see docs/MCP_TOOLS_COMPLETE.md for the complete list and full params/examples. The MCP JSON-RPC surface also supports the generic tools/list and tools/call endpoints to enumerate/invoke tools.)

Snipara has two pricing families:

1) Context Plans (Context Optimization)
- FREE — $0 / mo — 100 queries / month  
- PRO — $19 / mo — 5,000 queries / month  
- TEAM — $49 / mo — 20,000 queries / month  
- ENTERPRISE — $499 / mo — Unlimited queries / month

2) Agents Plans (Memory & Swarms)
- STARTER — $15 / mo (no prerequisite)  
- PRO — $39 / mo (no prerequisite)  
- TEAM — $79 / mo (requires Context TEAM or higher)  
- ENTERPRISE — $199 / mo (requires Context ENTERPRISE only)

Note: Agents TEAM/ENTERPRISE are team-level features and require the matching Context plan.

Short summary
- Snipara is split across three repositories that separate the web frontend + production MCP server, the lightweight PyPI client, and an enterprise/self‑hosted mirror repo. This lets the team run a single production deploy while maintaining a standalone client package and an enterprise sync target.

Three components
1. Monorepo: alopez3006/snipara-webapp (production source)
   - Contains: apps/web (Next.js 14 web app), apps/mcp-server (Python FastAPI MCP server), packages/database (JS Prisma schema and client), packages/shared, etc.
   - Production deploy: the MCP server for api.snipara.com is built from apps/mcp-server in this monorepo (Railway service `snipara-fastapi`).
   - Dockerfile in apps/mcp-server is used for production (model pre-download, multi‑worker gunicorn setup).

2. PyPI client repo: alopez3006/snipara-mcp (standalone)
   - Purpose: single-purpose PyPI client package (snipara-mcp).
   - Not deployed as a service; published to PyPI for users/clients to consume.

3. Enterprise / self-hosted mirror: Snipara/snipara-server (not the production source)
   - Purpose: enterprise/self‑hosted distribution and operations (local clone at /Users/alopez/Devs/snipara-fastapi).
   - Must be kept in sync for enterprise releases. Contains init-db.sh that runs dangerous `prisma db push --accept-data-loss` — hence careful syncing required.
   - Sync workflow: copy changed MCP server files from monorepo apps/mcp-server/src/* into the enterprise repo, commit, and push.

Important operational notes (from docs)
- Production deploys come from apps/mcp-server in the monorepo (use railway up -d from that folder).
- Certain files must always be synced to the enterprise repo: server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma.
- Prisma schema sync is critical: the JS schema (packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma) and the Python schema (apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma) share the same Neon DB; if they diverge the enterprise init script can drop columns. Always copy models/fields/enums to the Python schema after JS schema changes and push both.
- Docker/Deployment specifics: pre-download embedding model at build time, preload in FastAPI lifespan, run multiple gunicorn workers (avoid Railway startCommand override), health/readiness endpoints (/health, /ready).

This three-repo architecture separates production service code and web UI (monorepo), the lightweight client package (PyPI repo), and the enterprise/self-hosted distribution (mirror repo) while requiring careful sync of critical server and Prisma schema files.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a fixed token budget per context query (client-set via max_tokens or project default) and returns an OPTIMIZED CONTEXT that fits that budget by searching, ranking, and selecting/compressing document chunks until the token budget is exhausted.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Caller invokes a query (rlm_context_query / rlm_ask) with a max_tokens or uses the project's default maxTokensPerQuery (default ~4000).
2. Snipara runs a search (keyword / semantic / hybrid) over pre‑computed DocumentChunks (each chunk stores tokenCount) and/or on‑the‑fly embeddings for fresh text.
3. Results are scored and ranked. Shared-context documents and project documents are considered, and the token budget is split between sources (example: 60% project docs + 40% shared context).
4. Snipara iteratively selects the highest‑scoring sections and sums their tokenCount until the max_tokens budget is reached. It applies compression strategies where appropriate:
   - Uses precomputed DocumentSummaries when available
   - Performs chunking or compression to shrink content to fit the remaining budget
5. Returns the OPTIMIZED CONTEXT (selected sections + metadata), and includes budget telemetry (total_tokens, budget_allocation).
6. Client passes that context to their LLM for inference.

Key policies and defaults you should know
- Workflow budgets:
  - LITE mode: ~3–5K tokens (typical bug/one‑file tasks)
  - FULL mode: ~8–15K tokens (multi‑file/architectural work)
- Project default: maxTokensPerQuery (default ~4000) lives on the Project record.
- Shared context allocation: shared docs are allocated a fixed portion of the budget (example proportions and a returned budget_allocation object show exact token splits). Shared document categories are prioritized (MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% by policy).
- Chunks and summaries track tokenCount so selection is token-aware (DocumentChunk.tokenCount, DocumentSummary.tokenCount).

Performance & safety controls
- Hard caps and heuristics (e.g., max candidates, keyword score drop‑off) prevent huge embedding batches and timeouts.
- Two embedding paths: precomputed chunks (preferred) and faster on‑the‑fly embedding (uses a smaller/faster model) when needed.
- If Snipara hits errors/timeouts, callers should fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob per the non‑negotiable rules.

Observability and billing
- Query usage is tracked (inputTokens, outputTokens, latencyMs) for billing/analytics.
- Responses include total_tokens used and budget_allocation so you can audit what was returned.

Practical examples
- rlm_context_query(query="auth", max_tokens=6000) → Snipara will allocate tokens between project docs and shared context (e.g., 60/40), pick highest‑ranked chunks (by tokenCount) and compress as needed so the returned context totals ≤6000 tokens.
- For team sessions you can pre-load team standards (rlm_shared_context) and Snipara will reserve the configured share of the token budget for those documents automatically.

Gotchas / risks
- Shared docs with forced high relevance can crowd out project docs unless their relevance/priority is tuned.
- Large ad‑hoc on‑the‑fly embedding batches can hit timeouts; the system uses caps and faster models to mitigate but prefer precomputed chunks.
- Keep DocumentChunk and DocumentSummary token counts accurate (they drive selection).

If you want, I can show an example rlm_context_query call and the typical returned budget_allocation/sections payload.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team- or global-scoped bundles of reusable documents (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, prompt templates) that projects can inherit automatically. They let teams supply non‑project context that Snipara will include in context‑optimization queries so implementations automatically follow team rules.

How they work (high level)
1. A Team Admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads SharedDocuments into it (rlm_upload_shared_document).
2. Each SharedDocument has a category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) and a priority (0–100). These determine importance and token allocation.
3. Collections are linked to projects (via the dashboard). Once linked, Snipara automatically includes shared documents when a project uses rlm_context_query or other MCP tools.
4. Token budgets are split between project docs and shared context (example default: 60% project docs, 40% shared context). Within shared context the budget is allocated roughly 40% MANDATORY / 30% BEST_PRACTICES / 20% GUIDELINES / 10% REFERENCE.
5. When you call rlm_context_query for a project, Snipara returns an optimized set of sections that includes shared‑context docs ranked and truncated to the allocated token budget. You can also load shared context separately with rlm_shared_context and inject it into a session with rlm_inject.

Key properties
- Purpose: enforce team standards, make best practices immediately available, and reduce the need to copy rules into every project.
- Scope: GLOBAL, TEAM, or USER collections; projects inherit documents from linked team collections.
- Categories: MANDATORY (highest), BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE (lowest).
- Priority: numeric 0–100 to order docs within a category.
- Prompt templates: stored in collections and accessible via rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template.

Common commands / API flow
- rlm_list_collections(include_public=true) — list available collections
- rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id, title, content, category, priority) — add doc
- rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) — retrieve shared docs (pre-allocated budgets returned)
- rlm_inject(context=..., append=false) — inject shared context into session
- rlm_context_query(query="...", max_tokens=...) — project query automatically includes linked shared context

Best practices
- Start with MANDATORY rules (security/arch). Add BEST_PRACTICES gradually.
- Keep each shared document focused (one topic per doc).
- Use priority to rank important documents within a category.
- Use prompt templates for consistent reviews and PR checks.
- Load MANDATORY + BEST_PRACTICES at session start (rlm_shared_context → rlm_inject) so they apply to all subsequent queries.

Known pitfalls / risks
- Shared docs with an artificially high hardcoded relevance score can outrank relevant project docs. Avoid hardcoding relevance_score=1.0.
- Non‑MANDATORY docs need at least one keyword in the title to be included (Snipara uses title-only matching and stop-word filters for some shared-context fallbacks).
- If you need stricter enforcement, inject MANDATORY docs into the session (rlm_inject) so they are always present in context.

Example usage pattern
1. Team admin: rlm_upload_shared_document(..., category="MANDATORY", priority=90)
2. Developer session start:
   - shared = rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"])
   - rlm_inject(context=shared_doc_text, append=false)
3. Run: context = rlm_context_query("implement X") → Snipara returns project + shared context (token split applied)
4. Implement following the returned context and remember decisions with rlm_remember.

Access
- Team plan features: Shared Collections are available to Team and Enterprise plans. Collections can be public or team‑private and are linked to projects in the dashboard so projects automatically inherit them.

If you want, I can:
- show a concrete example of uploading a TypeScript coding standard doc and how it appears in rlm_shared_context output, or
- draft a short MANDATORY doc template your team can upload.

Short answer
OAuth device flow in Snipara is the standard device‑authorization flow: the client requests a device code, shows the user a short user_code + verification URL, polls the token endpoint until the user authorizes, then receives an access (and optional refresh) token. Snipara stores tokens hashed and exposes them via Bearer authorization (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...).

Detailed steps (client + server behavior)
1. Client requests a device code
   - POST to the device-code endpoint (use the API host with www — see Pitfall below).
   - Server returns a DeviceCode record containing:
     - deviceCode (long string)
     - userCode (short human code shown to user)
     - verification URL (where the user authorizes)
     - interval (default polling interval, e.g., 5s)
     - expires_in
   - Server creates a DeviceCode row with status = PENDING.

2. Show instructions to the user
   - Display the verification URL and userCode for the user to open in a browser and sign in/authorize.

3. User authorizes in the browser
   - User visits the verification URL, signs in, and approves the requested scopes.
   - Server updates the DeviceCode status to AUTHORIZED (or DENIED/EXPIRED if cancelled/timeouts).

4. Client polls the token endpoint
   - Client repeatedly POSTs to the token endpoint with deviceCode at the specified interval.
   - Polling continues until:
     - Server returns tokens (status AUTHORIZED) → success
     - Server returns an error (DENIED, EXPIRED, or timeout) → fail
   - Typical DeviceCode statuses: PENDING, AUTHORIZED, EXPIRED, USED, DENIED.

5. Exchange and usage
   - When authorized, server returns an access token (and usually a refresh token).
   - Snipara stores access/refresh tokens as hashed values; tokenPrefix (first 12 chars) is kept for display/identification.
   - Use the access token in API calls: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Respect accessExpiresAt and refreshExpiresAt; refresh as needed.

Server data model (from docs)
- DeviceCode model fields: deviceCode, userCode, status, interval (default 5s).
- OAuthToken model fields: accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash, tokenPrefix, scope, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt.

Important pitfalls & best practices
- Always use the www host for POST endpoints. Using snipara.com causes a 301 → www.snipara.com which can convert POST → GET and silently fail (common cause of 405 errors).
- Use the returned polling interval (default 5s) — do not poll faster.
- Handle all DeviceCode statuses (DENIED, EXPIRED, USED) gracefully.
- Treat tokens as sensitive; the server only stores hashes. Use the tokenPrefix only for display/lookup.
- For API calls, include the header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...

Minimal example (conceptual)
- Request device code → show user_code and verification URL
- Poll token endpoint every interval seconds
- On success: store access token client‑side and use Authorization: Bearer <token>

If you want, I can draft example curl requests for the device-code and token endpoints (using www.snipara.com) or show sample client polling logic. Which would be most useful?

Short answer — the core/primary DB models (from the schema reference) are:

Core models
- User — accounts created via NextAuth (id, name, email, registrationSource, etc.)
- Team — organizations owning projects (name, slug, isPersonal, sso settings)
- Project — documentation projects (slug, searchMode, maxTokensPerQuery, etc.)
- Document — indexed markdown files (path, content, hash, projectId)
- DocumentChunk — chunked embeddings for semantic search (content, embedding, tokenCount, start/end lines)
- DocumentSummary — LLM-generated summaries (summary, summaryType, tokenCount)

Authentication / API
- ApiKey — project-scoped API keys (hashed key, prefix, expiresAt, revokedAt)
- TeamApiKey — team-scoped API keys (same shape, scoped to team)
- OAuthToken — stored OAuth access/refresh token hashes
- DeviceCode — device-flow codes (deviceCode, userCode, status, interval)

Billing & Usage
- Subscription — team context plan (plan, status, stripe ids)
- AgentsSubscription — billing for Agents features (plan, memoryLimit, swarmLimit)
- Query (usage) — MCP tool call tracking (tool, input/output tokens, latency, success, projectId)

Shared context & templates
- SharedContextCollection — reusable collections of standards/guidelines
- SharedDocument — documents inside a collection (category, priority)
- PromptTemplate — stored templates with variables

Agents & caching
- AgentMemory — persistent agent memories (type, scope, confidence, accessCount)
- QueryCache — L2 cache for context query results (queryHash, sections, totalTokens)
- AgentSwarm — multi-agent coordination (name, maxAgents, isActive)

Permissions / Team config
- TeamPermissionConfig — fine-grained permissions config (mode, projectAccessControlEnabled)
- ProjectMember — per-project access levels (NONE, VIEWER, EDITOR, ADMIN)

(There are also many enums and auxiliary models referenced in the docs, but the list above covers the main database models.)

Snipara uses a three-tier (layered) architecture: Presentation → Application → Data.

- Presentation layer: apps/web (Next.js 14 web app)
- Application/API layer: apps/mcp-server (Python FastAPI MCP server) + snipara-mcp client
- Data/persistence layer: packages/database (Prisma schema & client) backed by Neon PostgreSQL

This separation is reflected in the repo layout (“three repositories, one production deploy”) and the deployment (web app, MCP server, and shared database).

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query when you need relevant documentation/code context for a specific question or implementation step.
- Use rlm_decompose when the task is complex and needs to be broken into smaller, ordered sub-tasks (chunks) before you start implementing.

When to pick which (decision checklist)
- Quick / single-file / known-location work (LITE mode, score 0–1): rlm_context_query
  - Example: "How does authentication header get parsed?" or "Fix bug in oauth handler in file X"
  - Token budget: ~3–5K. Try rlm_ask for very small quick queries.
- Multi-file / architectural / multi-session work (FULL mode, score 2+): rlm_decompose (after rlm_plan)
  - Example: "Implement OAuth integration across DB, config, endpoints, refresh logic"
  - Workflow: rlm_plan → rlm_decompose → for each chunk call rlm_context_query → implement with RLM-Runtime
  - Token budget: ~8–15K.

What each tool gives you
- rlm_context_query
  - Purpose: semantic + keyword search that returns the optimized context sections for a single query (sections, scores, token-count).
  - Use for: reading docs/code relevant to a concrete question or single implementation step; iterative, chunk-by-chunk work.
  - Typical follow-up: edit code, run tests, possibly call rlm_context_query again for the next small step.
- rlm_decompose
  - Purpose: take a complex query/task and split it into sub-queries / chunks, with dependencies and suggested execution order and budgets.
  - Use for: planning and orchestration — when you need an execution plan or to parallelize work across team members or steps.
  - Output: sub-queries, dependency graph, suggested token/context budgets per chunk.

How they work together (recommended pattern)
1. Decide mode (score). If FULL:
   - rlm_plan(query) to get the high-level plan.
   - rlm_decompose(query, max_depth=...) to break the plan into chunks with dependencies.
   - For each chunk:
     - Run rlm_context_query(chunk_query, max_tokens=...) to fetch optimized context.
     - Implement/test using RLM-Runtime or local editing.
2. If LITE:
   - Directly run rlm_context_query(task) → implement → test.

Practical tips
- If you only need a fast, predictable small context, use rlm_ask (quick ~2500 tokens).
- Use rlm_decompose when you need topological ordering, parallelization, or explicit chunk boundaries for RLM-Runtime.
- Keep token budgets in mind: rlm_context_query is for per-step context; rlm_decompose helps assign budgets across many steps.
- Use rlm_multi_query for parallel batch querying across multiple chunks/projects if needed.

Examples
- Bug fix in one file: rlm_context_query("fix null pointer in X controller")
- New feature spanning DB + API + tests: rlm_plan("Implement feature X") → rlm_decompose("feature X", max_depth=2) → per-chunk rlm_context_query(...)

If you want, tell me the specific task and I’ll recommend which to run first and with what options.

Short answer (TL;DR)
- Use hybrid for most documentation/code queries — it balances exact matches + semantic recall and is the safe default.
- Use keyword when you need precise, fast matches (file paths, identifiers, regex-like lookups) or when embeddings are unavailable.
- Use semantic for high-level, conceptual, design, or cross-project discovery where wording differs but meaning matters.

Why the three modes exist
- keyword: purely text/term matching (fast, deterministic). Great for exact names, file paths, code tokens.
- semantic: embedding-based similarity (higher recall for paraphrases). Great for conceptual questions, architecture, “how do we implement X?” across projects.
- hybrid: combines both scores so you get precise title/token matches plus semantic recall for paraphrases — best for most real-world doc/code queries.

When to pick each (rules of thumb + examples)
- Keyword
  - When: You know exact terms, file paths, function/class names, or need a deterministic match.
  - Examples: “Find occurrences of `rateLimiter.ts`”, “Where is NEXTAUTH_SECRET defined?”, “Search for SQL injection in repo”
  - Tools: rlm_search for regex/patterns, or rlm_context_query(search_mode="keyword") for budgeted results
  - Pros: Fast, low compute, interpretable. Cons: misses paraphrases or differently-named implementations.

- Semantic
  - When: You want concept-level matches, design rationale, or to find similar implementations across projects despite different wording.
  - Examples: “How do we implement rate limiting?”, “OAuth 2.0 integration patterns”, “Find systems that use token refresh logic”
  - Tools: rlm_context_query(search_mode="semantic") or rlm_multi_project_query with semantic ranking
  - Pros: High recall across wording differences. Cons: Slower, more compute, can return fuzzy matches — best with a reasonable token budget.

- Hybrid (recommended default)
  - When: Most documentation and feature-implementation queries, especially when you want both exact matches and related paraphrases.
  - Examples: “authentication implementation”, “implement user registration API endpoint”, chunk-by-chunk feature work
  - Tools: rlm_context_query(search_mode="hybrid") — example used throughout the docs and feature workflows
  - Pros: Best balance of precision + recall. Cons: Slightly more compute than pure keyword, but usually worth it.

Practical guidance & patterns
- Default: Start with hybrid for rlm_context_query. The docs and workflows use hybrid by default (e.g., feature implementation examples).
- Fast fallback: If the MCP server is failing or embeddings are unavailable, fall back to keyword/local Read/Grep/Glob.
- Code vs prose: Prefer keyword/regex for exact code patterns; prefer semantic/hybrid for design/docs/questions.
- Cross-project discovery: Use semantic or hybrid with rlm_multi_project_query to find implementations across team projects.
- Shared context: Hybrid helps include both team standards (title/keyword matches) and relevant project sections. Be aware non-MANDATORY shared docs may need a keyword match in the title to rank highly.
- Token budget: For quick tasks use the LITE budgets (~3–5K tokens). For chunked/full feature work use higher budgets (6K+ or 8–15K) and hybrid/semantic as needed.
- Performance caveats: Semantic searches use embeddings (more memory/CPU). If you have many sections and embeddings aren’t precomputed, search can time out — hybrid + candidate caps mitigate this.

Example calls
- Hybrid (general/feature work):
  rlm_context_query(query="implement user registration API endpoint", max_tokens=6000, search_mode="hybrid")
- Keyword (exact code search):
  rlm_context_query(query="NEXTAUTH_SECRET", max_tokens=1500, search_mode="keyword")
  or rlm_search(pattern="NEXTAUTH_SECRET", files=["apps/**"])
- Semantic (conceptual, cross-project):
  rlm_multi_project_query(query="How is OAuth implemented?", project_ids=[], max_tokens=8000, search_mode="semantic")

When to switch modes during a session
- Start hybrid. If results are too noisy and you need exact matches, switch to keyword.
- If hybrid/keyword miss related designs across repos, switch to semantic or run a multi-project semantic query.

Quick checklist
- I know exact identifiers or file paths → keyword
- I need conceptually similar results or cross-project discovery → semantic
- I want both precise matches and related results → hybrid (default)
- MCP errors or embedding cold-starts → fallback to local grep/Read/rlm_search

If you want, tell me an example query you have and I’ll recommend the exact mode + a sample rlm_context_query invocation.

Plan:
- List every auth method documented
- For each: explain how it is presented to the API, where it is stored/managed, and key security properties/risks
- Give short best-practice recommendations

Authentication options (what’s available)
- Project API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_<...>
  - Scope: single project
  - DB model: ApiKey (keyHash stored, keyPrefix visible), has expiresAt and revokedAt
- Team API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_<...>
  - Scope: all projects in a team
  - DB model: TeamApiKey (same shape as ApiKey, but team-scoped)
- OAuth (Device Flow)
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_<...>
  - Flow: device authorization flow (device code polling), returns access + refresh tokens
  - DB model: OAuthToken (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash, tokenPrefix, scope, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt)
  - DeviceCode object: stores deviceCode, userCode, status (PENDING, AUTHORIZED, EXPIRED, USED, DENIED), and polling interval
- End-user sign-in via NextAuth (web auth)
  - Providers: GitHub, Google, Email (NextAuth.js)
  - Uses session cookies / NextAuth sessions for browser-based login; maps to User/Team membership
- OAuth tokens for third-party clients (same as device flow tokens, but general OAuth usage)
- (Implicit) Session-based/admin console logins and API clients authenticated by those sessions (backed by NextAuth and session tables)
- Note: all token/key types are bearer credentials — possession = access.

How they differ in security (concise)
- Scope / blast radius
  - Team API Key: broadest access (all team projects) → highest blast radius if leaked
  - Project API Key: limited to a single project → lower blast radius, preferred for least privilege
  - OAuth access tokens: scoped by OAuth scopes (e.g., mcp:read mcp:write) and limited lifetime → fine-grained least privilege if scopes are used correctly
  - NextAuth sessions: tied to user identity and web session controls (refresh/expiration, CSRF protections) → better for interactive user actions
- Lifetimes & revocation
  - API keys: can have expiresAt and revokedAt; until revoked/expired they are valid
  - OAuth tokens: accessExpiresAt + refreshExpiresAt; refresh token rotation can reduce risk
  - Device codes: short-lived, single-use; reduce risk for device auth
- Storage & leakage protection
  - Keys/tokens in DB are hashed (keyHash, accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash); only prefixes stored for display → helps if DB is leaked
  - Bearer tokens in transit must be protected by TLS; anyone with token can call APIs
- Usability vs security tradeoff
  - API keys are simple to use (server-to-server) but are bearer tokens with full access of their scope — higher operational risk if not rotated/limited
  - OAuth device flow is safer for client apps because it is user-consented and tokens expire/refresh; suited for user-level access
  - NextAuth (browser) benefits from session protections (CSRF, cookie flags), but is not suitable for server-to-server automation
- Auditability & monitoring
  - All calls generate Query usage records (tool, tokens, latency, projectId) — you can monitor and detect misuse
- Implementation pitfalls noted in docs
  - Always use www.snipara.com for POST endpoints (device flow POST → snipara.com causes 301 redirect which can convert POST→GET and break device flow)
  - Avoid embedding long-lived team keys in public clients or browser code
  - Keep token and key lifecycle policies (expiry, rotation, revoke) in place to reduce risk

Practical recommendations (best practices)
- Use the narrowest-scope credential possible (project API key over team key; OAuth tokens with minimal scopes)
- Set expirations on API keys and prefer short-lived OAuth access tokens with refresh tokens
- Store and transmit only over TLS; never hard-code long-lived keys into public clients
- Rotate keys regularly and revoke immediately on suspected compromise
- Use audit logs / rlm usage tracking to detect unusual activity
- For device flow clients: use the www.snipara.com POST endpoint to avoid redirect-induced failures

Short answer
- Snipara splits the query token budget between project docs and shared context, then allocates the shared portion across the shared-context categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) using fixed weights.

Details (how it works)
- Default split between project vs shared context:
  - 60% of the query token budget → project documents
  - 40% → shared context documents (this is the default behavior when rlm_context_query runs)
- Shared-context category allocation (percentage of the shared-context portion):
  - MANDATORY: 40%
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
  - GUIDELINES: 20%
  - REFERENCE: 10%
- Within each category:
  - Documents are ordered by category priority (the document’s priority 0–100) and semantic relevance.
  - Documents have a precomputed tokenCount and Snipara includes sections until that category’s token sub-budget is exhausted.
- Matching/filtering rules that affect inclusion:
  - Non-MANDATORY shared docs generally must match at least one keyword in the query title/body to be considered (title-only matching, stop-word filters, stemming fallback).
  - Shared docs with hardcoded high relevance can outrank project docs; use priority and focused docs to avoid noise.
- How to observe/control it:
  - Call rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) to fetch shared docs and see the returned budget_allocation and total_tokens.
  - rlm_context_query(max_tokens=N) will automatically include shared context and enforce the 60/40 split (unless overridden by project config).
  - You can influence results by restricting categories, adjusting max_tokens, or injecting session context (rlm_inject) for overrides.

Numeric example
- If max_tokens = 6000:
  - Project docs get 60% = 3600 tokens
  - Shared context gets 40% = 2400 tokens
    - MANDATORY = 40% of 2400 = 960 tokens
    - BEST_PRACTICES = 30% = 720 tokens
    - GUIDELINES = 20% = 480 tokens
    - REFERENCE = 10% = 240 tokens
  Snipara fills each bucket with the highest-priority / most-relevant shared documents until each bucket’s tokens are used.

Risks / best practices
- Mis-prioritized or overly-broad shared docs can push out relevant project context — keep shared documents focused and use the priority field.
- If you need a different distribution for a specific task, restrict which categories are loaded (rlm_shared_context) or inject explicit session context (rlm_inject) instead of relying on automatic inclusion.

References
- See docs/SHARED_CONTEXT.md and the rlm_shared_context / rlm_context_query examples for API usage and returned budget_allocation fields.

Short answer
- Snipara does not include a built-in, first-class real‑time (OT/CRDT-style) collaborative editor in the product docs. The platform provides team collaboration primitives (teams, projects, shared context collections, GitHub sync, VS Code extension) that you can combine for multi‑user workflows, or you can add real‑time editing by extending the web app (apps/web) and the backend (apps/mcp-server) in a self‑hosted/enterprise deployment.

Recommended options (least effort → full custom)

1) Use GitHub + Snipara sync + PRs (recommended for most teams)
- Enable githubSyncEnabled on a Project (Project.githubSyncEnabled true).
- Team members edit in normal Git workflows (branches / PR review).
- Snipara indexes the repository and provides context queries, plan/decompose, and shared context for reviews.
Why: no extra infra, works with existing Snipara indexing and code-review flows.

2) Pair programming / live editing via VS Code Live Share + Snipara VS Code extension
- Use VS Code Live Share (or similar) for real-time editing sessions.
- Install Snipara VS Code Extension (ext install snipara.snipara) so participants have Snipara commands, workspace doc scanner, and MCP tooling available during the session.
Why: real-time editing provided by Live Share; Snipara tooling stays available in-editor without product changes.

3) Use the web app + presence features (partial)
- Use shared context collections (rlm_shared_context) and prompt templates to coordinate edits and enforce standards.
- Use rlm_remember/rlm_recall to log session decisions and progress for team visibility.
Why: good for coordination and traceability, but not true low-latency co-editing.

4) Build true real‑time collaboration (enterprise / self-hosted)
- If you must have OT/CRDT real‑time editing, implement it in the web app (apps/web) and add server-side support (apps/mcp-server).
- Typical approach:
  - Choose a CRDT/OT library (Yjs / Automerge / ShareDB).
  - Add presence and document synchronization endpoints (WebSocket / WebRTC) to apps/mcp-server or a dedicated realtime service.
  - Store canonical document state or deltas in your PostgreSQL/Prisma layer (add models for presence / collaborative sessions).
  - Ensure indexing: update document indexing pipeline so Snipara re-indexes committed content or snapshots.
  - Use authentication: integrate with Snipara teams/projects and ApiKey/TeamApiKey models for auth.
Why: full product capability, but requires engineering and infra.

If you decide to build (files and steps)
- Files to change (monorepo):
  - apps/web/ — add CRDT client integration, presence UI, and session UI
  - apps/mcp-server/src/ — add WebSocket/WebRTC endpoints and presence/session services (new services/*.py)
  - packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma — add models for collaborative sessions, presence, and deltas (then sync to Python Prisma schema)
  - docs/ — update user documentation and admin instructions for collaborative editing
- Key steps
  1. Pick CRDT/OT implementation (Yjs recommended for JS clients).
  2. Add a realtime service or extend apps/mcp-server with WebSockets.
  3. Add Prisma models for collaborative sessions (session id, document id, participants, last_synced_snapshot, delta store).
  4. Update indexing pipeline so new edits/snapshots trigger rlm_upload_document or re-indexing.
  5. Add permission checks using Project/Team membership and ApiKey/TeamApiKey rules.
  6. Add tests and CI (mcp-server CI uses pytest + pgvector).
  7. Sync Prisma schemas (JS ↔ Python) — see "Prisma Schema Sync (CRITICAL)".

Important operational notes & risks (from docs)
- Prisma schema sync: after adding DB models, copy changes to apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma; failing to sync can drop columns on enterprise init-db.sh. Always pnpm db:push and redeploy as described in docs.
- Deployment quirks:
  - Do NOT add a Railway startCommand that would bypass multi-worker gunicorn (causes single-worker zombies).
  - Model pre-download / preload is required for embedding service readiness; ensure readiness healthchecks remain valid.
- Performance & scaling:
  - Real-time session traffic needs WebSocket scaling and careful database writes (store snapshots/deltas efficiently).
  - Keep indexing and embedding cost in mind — frequent small edits should not force full re-embedding of large documents.
- Security / permissions:
  - Enforce project-level permissions using Project and Team models and ApiKey/TeamApiKey.
  - Audit and rate-limit collaborative sessions to avoid abuse.
- Patch-only policy (internal dev practice):
  - If you’re adding real-time features as a quick fix, avoid large refactors; propose architectural changes first.

Quick verification commands (use after changes)
- Test liveness/readiness:
  - curl -s https://api.snipara.com/health
  - curl -s https://api.snipara.com/ready
- Test MCP tools:
  - tools/list and tools/call as shown in docs to verify server + engine paths.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces the monthly query quota for your Context plan. Once you exceed your plan’s allowance, additional MCP context queries are rejected (you’ll receive an error indicating quota/limit exceeded) until either the quota resets or you upgrade.

What this means (details)
- Plan limits (monthly): FREE = 100, PRO = 5,000, TEAM = 20,000, ENTERPRISE = Unlimited. (See Pricing Plans.)
- All MCP calls are tracked (Query usage stored for billing/analytics), so over‑use is detected and enforced.
- The exact client-visible error depends on the MCP response your client receives (typically an error/HTTP status indicating quota exceeded). The request will not return optimized context while over quota.

What you can do
1. Check usage
   - Use the MCP/stat tool (rlm_stats) or your dashboard to view current usage. Example (from docs):
     - Call tools/call rlm_stats via the MCP endpoint to get usage details.
2. Upgrade or change plan
   - Upgrade to a higher Context plan (PRO / TEAM / ENTERPRISE) in the dashboard to increase or remove limits.
3. Enterprise / Team options
   - Team and Enterprise plans have higher quotas (TEAM = 20k) or unlimited (ENTERPRISE). For agents features you also need the appropriate Context plan.
4. Contact support / billing
   - If you need a custom quota or temporary increase, contact Sales/Billing via the dashboard (or your account rep) to request changes.

Caveat / risk
- The docs don’t specify the exact HTTP status code or error message returned on quota breach, so client behavior may vary; handle quota errors in your client gracefully (show upgrade prompts, back off, or retry after reset).

Short answer
- rlm_context_query will return a successful response with no matching sections (empty results) — this is not a 500 error.
- Your workflow should then try broader/alternate searches or fall back to other Snipara MCP tools and, only if needed, local Read/Grep/Glob.

Recommended steps (in order)
1. Verify result and ask a clarifying question to the user (if query may be ambiguous).
2. Re-run Snipara with adjusted parameters:
   - broaden the query terms, remove stop words, or increase max_tokens
   - try a different search_mode (hybrid / semantic / keyword)
   Example: rlm_context_query(query="authentication oauth token refresh", max_tokens=6000, search_mode="hybrid")
3. Search other Snipara tools:
   - rlm_search for regex / path searches (if you know filenames/patterns)
   - rlm_multi_project_query to search across all projects
   - rlm_shared_context to include team standards/best practices
   - rlm_sections or rlm_stats to browse project structure and available docs
4. If the project is missing docs, prompt to upload/index them (rlm_upload_document) or advise the owner to sync from GitHub.
5. If Snipara returns an error (500/timeout) instead of empty: fall back immediately to local Read/Grep/Glob tools per the non‑negotiable rules.

Useful example commands
- rlm_context_query(...):
  rlm_context_query(query="user registration authentication", max_tokens=4000, search_mode="hybrid")
- fallback regex search:
  rlm_search(query="find:password|oauth|token", path="src/**")
- team-wide search:
  rlm_multi_project_query(query="How do we handle OAuth?", project_ids=[], max_tokens=8000)
- inspect docs list:
  rlm_sections(max_tokens=2000) or rlm_stats(project_id="current")

When to fall back to local tools
- Snipara returned an error (500, timeout) → immediately use local Read/Grep/Glob.
- You know the exact file/path to read → use local Read(file_path=...) (no search needed).
- You must edit or run commands (Snipara is read-only) → use local tooling.

If nothing is found
- Tell the user no docs were found, propose clarifying questions, offer to search codebase, and offer to upload or index missing docs so future queries succeed.

Risk
- Broadening search can return low‑precision results; prefer iterative clarification and use rlm_shared_context or rlm_sections to narrow scope before returning context.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it massively reduces and compacts a customer’s documentation into a small, highly relevant context bundle (≈90% reduction, e.g., 500K → ~5K tokens) so clients can get accurate LLM answers within token limits using their own model. 

Key benefits:
- Delivers optimized context (not LLM responses) tailored to a client’s token budget  
- LLM-agnostic — no vendor lock-in (clients use their own LLM)  
- Low-cost, high-margin service (compute-only; no inference costs passed through)

Snipara is built as a simple three‑component system. At a high level those components are:

1. Client (snipara-mcp / MCP clients)
   - The user‑facing SDK/CLI and MCP clients (pip package `snipara-mcp`, uvx, local stdio/HTTP clients).
   - Runs in the user/agent environment and issues MCP requests (HTTP or stdio) using a .mcp.json configuration.
   - Responsible for calling Snipara tools (rlm_context_query, rlm_ask, rlm_search, etc.) and sending documents/queries to the server.

2. MCP Server (snipara-fastapi)
   - The FastAPI backend that implements authentication, the MCP transport, tool endpoints, and the context‑optimization engine (files: server.py, auth.py, mcp_transport.py, rlm_engine.py, etc.).
   - Exposes the MCP HTTP endpoints (api.snipara.com/mcp/...) and executes the toolset, semantic search, token budgeting and other optimization logic.
   - Stores/reads data and enforces auth/usage rules. Deployed on Railway (requires the Railway CLI `railway up`; monorepo changes must be synced to the separate snipara-fastapi repo).

3. Infrastructure & Web UI (storage services + web app)
   - Persistent services: PostgreSQL and Redis for document storage, sessions, and caching.
   - Web application (deployed e.g. on Vercel) for front‑end/management tasks and for serving the broader product surface.
   - Orchestrates and exposes higher‑level product features; interacts with the MCP server for API calls.

How they interact (workflow)
- The client issues an MCP request (HTTP or stdio) per .mcp.json.
- The MCP server authenticates the request, runs the rlm_engine/tools, reads/writes data to Postgres/Redis, and returns optimized context/results.
- The web UI and other services use the same MCP server and backing stores for admin, UX, and integrations.

This separation makes Snipara LLM‑agnostic (clients/agents supply the LLM), concentrates optimization and tooling in the FastAPI MCP server, and keeps persistence and UI concerns in the infrastructure/web layer.

Short version
- Snipara measures and enforces a hard token budget when building context for LLM calls. It counts tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranks candidate sections by relevance, then greedily selects the highest‑relevance sections until the token budget is used. If only part of a section fits, it smart‑truncates at a sentence boundary, marks that section truncated, adds "..." and sets the overall truncated flag.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Token counting
   - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to compute token counts for document sections and the running total.
   - All budgeting decisions use token counts (not characters or words).

2. Candidate retrieval & ranking
   - Sections are gathered by keyword, semantic, or hybrid search (rlm_context_query supports search_mode: keyword|semantic|hybrid).
   - Sections are scored for relevance and sorted (higher relevance first).

3. Greedy selection within budget
   - Iterate ranked sections, add full sections while total_tokens + section_tokens <= max_tokens.
   - If a section would overflow the budget but some tokens remain, run smart_truncate() to produce a sentence‑bounded partial section that fits the remaining tokens and mark it truncated.
   - Stop when budget is exhausted or no more useful sections.

4. Smart truncation
   - Truncates at sentence boundaries (looks for . ! ?), avoids cutting mid‑sentence.
   - Appends "..." to indicate truncation.
   - Records truncated: true for that section and reflects truncated: true in the overall response if budget was filled.

5. Session & system context
   - Session context can be injected into the response (implementation note: include up to ~20% of the budget reserved for session context).
   - System instructions can be auto‑injected as part of the process.

6. Metadata & response shape
   - The returned structure includes per‑section metadata: title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count, truncated flag when applicable.
   - Also returns total_tokens, overall truncated boolean, session_context if any, and suggestions (related docs).

Policy/priority & allocation layers
- Category and project allocations: Snipara supports priority and category allocation rules (examples shown: CATEGORY_BUDGETS: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%; web defaults include project: 40% plus shared categories).
- Frontend/server allocation utilities group docs (project vs shared categories) and allocate sub‑budgets per category, then perform selection within each category (again by relevance and token count).
- Hard limits and thresholds exist (e.g., max tokens per collection, min relevance threshold) to enforce caps.

Tools & integrations
- Primary tool: rlm_context_query (params: query, max_tokens, search_mode, include_metadata, optional session_id). It implements token budgeting logic and returns the full metadata response.
- Support tools: decompose and multi_query which use the same budgeting principles to split work or run parallel context queries.
- Models/schemas exist for parameters and responses (Pydantic models for ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult).

Other notes
- Token budgeting is independent of calling an LLM (counts and truncation are pure local logic).
- The system records usage metrics (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache hits) alongside the response.
- This approach lets Snipara optimize context size (compression_ratio / token_reduction_pct) and control cost while preserving the most relevant information.

Shared Context Collections are reusable, team- or org-level document collections (coding standards, architecture rules, templates, examples) that are linked into projects so every developer and every AI query automatically has access to the same team rules and guidance.

How they work (high level)
- Create a collection (team admin) and upload documents (rules, best practices, templates).
- Link that shared collection to one or more projects in the dashboard.
- When a project query runs, Snipara automatically merges project documents and linked shared collections and injects the shared context into the model prompt.
- The merged context is prioritized and token-budgeted by document category so the most important rules are always included.

Key concepts
- Categories (priority and token budget):
  - MANDATORY — highest priority (40% token budget): non‑negotiable rules (security, architecture).
  - BEST_PRACTICES — high (30%): recommended patterns and conventions.
  - GUIDELINES — medium (20%): helpful suggestions.
  - REFERENCE — low (10%): background info and examples.
- Token budgeting: when rlm_shared_context or rlm_context_query is called, tokens are split across project docs and shared context (commonly 60%/40%), and within shared context by category percentages above.
- Project-specific docs always take precedence over shared docs; collections themselves have ordering you can reorder to change priority.
- Conflict handling: linking a collection can surface conflicts which can be acknowledged and resolved via the UI (Project Context Manager supports linking/unlinking, reordering, and conflict modals).

Common tools / APIs
- rlm_list_collections — list available shared collections (team/private/public).
- rlm_upload_shared_document — upload a document into a collection (specify category and priority).
- rlm_shared_context — fetch merged shared context (filter categories, set max_tokens).
- rlm_context_query — query project context; shared context is automatically included when the project is linked.
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — list and retrieve prompt templates stored in collections.
- rlm_inject — inject shared context into session so it applies to subsequent queries.
- rlm_remember — record decisions (e.g., “implemented X following team standards”).

Typical workflow
1. Admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads MANDATORY and BEST_PRACTICES docs.
2. Admin links the collection to team projects (or all projects).
3. Developers call rlm_shared_context at session start (or the system injects it automatically).
4. All subsequent queries and code generation include team rules (e.g., “must include Zod validation”).
5. Use templates from collections for consistent PR reviews, specs, etc.
6. Record decisions with rlm_remember for future traceability.

When to use
- Enforce security/architecture rules across projects
- Ensure consistent code style, testing, and patterns
- Provide reusable prompt templates (code reviews, feature specs)
- Search across projects with consistent context (rlm_multi_project_query)

Access / plan notes
- Team/Enterprise plan feature (not available on Free/Pro).
- Enterprise adds additional capabilities (custom categories, analytics, unlimited multi-project queries).

Best practices for collections
- Start with MANDATORY rules (security, architecture).
- Add BEST_PRACTICES gradually.
- Use priority (0–100) within category to rank importance.
- Keep each document focused (one topic per doc).
- Update collections regularly and use templates for consistency.

Short version
- Snipara implements the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628). The user runs a CLI command, the CLI shows a verification URL and a short user code, the user opens the URL in a browser, signs in/approves the device, and the CLI polls the server until an access token is issued.

Detailed steps (how it works in Snipara)
1. CLI starts the device flow and shows the verification URL + code to the user (user visits URL and enters the code).
2. The CLI polls the token endpoint until the user has completed authorization:
   - Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/token
   - Poll request body example:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "abc123...",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While waiting the endpoint returns:
     { "error": "authorization_pending" }
   - When the user approves the device the endpoint returns a token response:
     {
       "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
       "token_type": "Bearer",
       "expires_in": 2592000,
       "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
     }
3. Access tokens use the prefix snipara_at_ and are validated on the server by a DB lookup + expiry check (see validate_oauth_token in auth.py). Scopes/read-write enforcement is applied.
4. Tokens are stored locally in ~/.snipara/tokens.json and used preferentially by the MCP client. If a token is expired or revoked you will see a 401:
   "Invalid or expired OAuth token. Re-authenticate at https://snipara.com/dashboard or run /snipara:quickstart"

How authentication is chosen by the client
- The client prefers stored OAuth tokens ( ~/.snipara/tokens.json ). If none or invalid, it falls back to API key authentication (X-API-Key: rlm_... or SNIPARA_API_KEY env var).

Troubleshooting / alternatives
- To skip OAuth and force API-key auth, set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment (the client then uses SNIPARA_API_KEY).
- Or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force API-key fallback: rm ~/.snipara/tokens.json (or mv ... .bak).

(Prisma models are PascalCase singular; their DB tables are mapped to snake_case plural — users, projects, team_members — and columns use camelCase by default.)

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (layered/import-hierarchy pattern). The layers (top → bottom) are:

- Routes (app/api/) — thin route handlers that authenticate, validate, and delegate
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters, lazy init + retry logic
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD and permission checks (never call Prisma directly outside here)
- Utils & Types — pure utilities and shared types, no dependencies

Key rule: lower layers cannot import from higher layers (enforces separation of concerns).

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation/code Q&A — single-topic semantic search, fast and cheap.
- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex and should be split into smaller sub-questions you want to inspect or run separately (power-user orchestration).

When to pick each (details)

1) rlm_context_query — pick this first for almost all doc/code lookups
- Best for single, focused queries: “How does authentication work?”, “Where is auth handled?”, “List API endpoints”.
- Returns optimized context within a token budget, includes shared best-practices first (configurable).
- Fast, fewer LLM calls and lower latency/cost than multi-step approaches.
- Example: call rlm_context_query with query="authentication" to get auth flow, session docs, file references.

2) rlm_decompose — pick this when the task is complex or multi-part
- Use when you need to break a large problem into sub-queries you can review or modify before executing (design, implementation plan, research across many topics).
- Good for orchestrating multi-step work: create a plan/sub-queries, then run those sub-queries in parallel (rlm_multi_query) or sequentially.
- Example uses: “Implement user authentication with email verification” → rlm_decompose returns ["login flow","JWT handling","email service","verification flow"]; then query each sub-topic.

How to combine them (recommended workflow)
1. For simple Q&A: call rlm_context_query directly.
2. For complex tasks:
   - Call rlm_decompose(query) to generate sub-queries.
   - Optionally review/edit the sub-queries.
   - Run rlm_multi_query or individual rlm_context_query calls on each sub-query.
   - Aggregate the results and act on them.

Tips and cautions
- rlm_decompose is a power-user tool — more orchestration but more control.
- Prefer rlm_context_query when you want a one-call authoritative context (lower cost/latency).
- Use rlm_ask for very quick, small queries when you don’t need the full context optimization.
- Remember shared context is included first by rlm_context_query (you can tune shared_context_budget_percent).

If you want, tell me an example query and I’ll recommend a concrete plan (use rlm_context_query now or decompose first).

Short guidance — which search mode to pick and why:

- Keyword (basic term matching, FREE)
  - Use when you know exact names/terms or need precise matches: specific function locations, error codes, API endpoint discovery, config values, quick grep-style lookups.
  - Fast, cheap, deterministic; works well for exact identifiers and pattern searches.

- Semantic (embedding similarity, PRO+)
  - Use for conceptual, explanatory, or architectural questions: “How does X work?”, system design, understanding flows, finding related concepts across docs.
  - Good when wording may differ from the source (synonyms, paraphrases) and you want concept-level matches.

- Hybrid (keyword + semantic, PRO+)
  - Use when you want the best of both: high recall for concepts plus precision on exact terms — e.g., general documentation searches, best-practices lookup, or when accuracy matters across a large codebase.
  - Recommended for broad searches where both relevance and exact references matter.

Quick mapping (from docs):
- Looking for specific function: keyword
- "How does X work?" questions: semantic
- General documentation search: hybrid
- Error code lookup: keyword
- Architecture understanding: semantic
- API endpoint discovery: keyword
- Best practices search: hybrid

Note: Free-plan requests for semantic/hybrid are automatically downgraded to keyword and include a warning flag (search_mode_downgraded: true).

All authentication methods in the system (what they are, how they are used, and the security differences):

1) OAuth 2.0 (Device Flow)
- What: Standard RFC 8628 device flow producing access tokens (prefix: snipara_at_) and refresh tokens (snipara_rt_). Client authenticates via a browser/code, server issues access_token, refresh_token, expiry and scopes.
- Where/headers: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... (MCP endpoints accept this).
- Storage and admin: Tokens are stored (client-side) in ~/.snipara/tokens.json and on the server as hashed values (accessTokenHash / refreshTokenHash). Tokens can be listed and revoked via /api/settings/tokens and DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id].
- Security properties:
  - Short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens → better for least-privilege and limiting exposure.
  - Scopes are enforced (read/write), enabling finer-grained permissions.
  - Tokens are individually revocable and logged minimally (tokenPrefix first chars).
  - Server-side and DB storage are hashed, reducing risk if DB is compromised.
  - Device flow reduces need to copy long-lived secrets.
  - Risk: leaked refresh tokens can re-issue access tokens until revoked/expired; client-side token files and server process caching can expose tokens unless removed and processes restarted.

2) API Keys
- What: Long-lived API keys with prefix rlm_. Server stores only SHA‑256 hashes of keys (lookup by hash).
- Where/headers: X-API-Key: rlm_... (MCP accepts X-API-Key; Inject API requires X-API-Key). Server code first tries a project key, then falls back to a team key.
- Storage and admin: Keys are hashed in DB, UI/logs only show limited prefix (first 8 chars).
- Security properties:
  - Good for automation and non‑interactive services.
  - Typically long-lived and therefore higher risk if leaked — unless rotated/revoked.
  - Less fine-grained than OAuth device-scope model (although access_level/project scoping exists).
  - Must be kept out of logs/source and stored in secure env/secret manager.

3) Combined behavior / server priority & quirks
- MCP server accepts both OAuth (Bearer) and API key (X-API-Key). It will prefer a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) and fall back to API key (env or header).
- The client/daemon may cache OAuth tokens in-process; removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json alone might not be sufficient — you may need to restart the client (claude) to force using the API key.
- Invalid/expired OAuth tokens return 401 with the re-authentication prompt.

Practical recommendations (security best practices)
- Prefer OAuth device flow for interactive users: short-lived tokens, scopes, per-client revocation.
- Use API keys for automated services but store them in secret managers, limit scope, rotate regularly, and revoke unused keys.
- Revoke tokens/keys via /api/settings/tokens (or dashboard) when compromised.
- If you need to force API-key auth on a client, remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json and restart the client to clear cached OAuth credentials.
- Monitor token usage and apply least privilege (scopes / project vs team keys).

Summary: OAuth (device-flow) is stronger for user interactions because of short lifetimes, scopes and revocability; API keys are convenient for automation but are higher-risk if long-lived and must be tightly guarded and rotated. Both token types are stored hashed server-side, which mitigates DB exposure.

Short answer
- The system splits the available token budget between shared context (team collections) and project docs, then divides the shared portion across categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).
- Within each bucket, documents are chosen by relevance (relevanceScore) and token size until that bucket’s token budget is exhausted.

How it works (concrete details)
1. Top-level split (project vs shared)
   - The budget is split into a “project” portion and a “shared” portion. This split is configurable (examples in the docs show 60% project / 40% shared or a parameter shared_context_budget_percent), but the server-side DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts sets project = 0.40 (40% of total) with the remaining 60% allocated to shared categories. You can override this via parameters or customAllocation.

2. Shared-category allocation
   - The shared portion is divided among categories. In the code default:
     - MANDATORY: 25%
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
     - GUIDELINES: 10%
     - REFERENCE: 5%
   - (Other doc examples show a 40/30/20/10 split for MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES/GUIDELINES/REFERENCE — treat these as documentation examples; the authoritative defaults are those in the token-budget.ts file unless overridden.)

3. Selection algorithm
   - Project docs are allocated first using the project budget.
   - For each shared category, allocateWithinBudget:
     - Sort docs by relevanceScore (if provided), then by priority/other ordering.
     - Add documents until tokensUsed + doc.tokenCount would exceed that category’s budget. Documents that don’t fit are marked allocated: false (exceeded budget).
   - The algorithm returns which documents are included and why (allocationReason).

4. Limits and enforcement
   - There are hard limits: max collections per project, max tokens per collection, max docs per collection, and a MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD.
   - If a collection exceeds MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, enforceCollectionLimits truncates that collection to fit the limit by including docs until the token cap is reached.

APIs / parameters you can use
- rlm_context_query accepts include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent to control whether shared context is included and how much budget it gets.
- rlm_shared_context supports filtering categories and max_tokens.
- The allocateTokenBudget function also accepts customAllocation to override defaults.

Practical takeaway
- Mandatory rules are prioritized and should be included whenever possible.
- Budgeting is configurable, but by default the token-budget.ts logic prioritizes project docs and then fills shared categories by relevance until each bucket is full.
- If you need a different split, pass shared_context_budget_percent or a customAllocation so the most important docs (e.g., MANDATORY) always get enough tokens.

Short answer
- Use a CRDT-based collaborative editor (Yjs or Automerge) layered on your chosen editor (Monaco or CodeMirror for code; TipTap/ProseMirror for docs), a websocket/awareness server for realtime presence, and a persistence layer that triggers Snipara re-indexing when documents change.
- Hook editor save/patch events -> persist CRDT state -> call your Snipara index/update API (Phase 0: Index docs) so rlm_context_query always gets the latest optimized context.
- Enforce auth/ACLs, presence/locking UX, and tests (LITE vs FULL modes) so collaborative edits flow into your Snipara workflow safely.

Why this approach
- CRDTs (Yjs/Automerge) give conflict-free realtime collaboration (no central OT complexity).
- Layering Yjs on Monaco/CodeMirror/TipTap gives a polished editing UX.
- Persisting CRDT updates and calling Snipara’s indexing keeps the context optimizer in sync so teams’ realtime edits are reflected in LLM queries.

Concrete implementation checklist

1) Pick editor + CRDT
- Code: Monaco or CodeMirror + Yjs (y-monaco, y-codemirror)
- Docs/rich text: TipTap/ProseMirror + Yjs

2) Collaboration server & awareness
- Use y-websocket (simple) or y-socket.io (if you need socket.io features); for peer-to-peer choose y-webrtc for small teams.
- Run the websocket server in your infra (k8s service or small VM). Add scaling (horizontal) behind sticky sessions or use a redis-based awareness adapter.

3) Persist CRDT state and changes
- Options: y-leveldb, file-based persistence, or persist serialized updates into Postgres/S3.
- On each persisted update (or on a short debounce), emit an indexing event to Snipara (see Phase 0: Index docs/*, README.md, CLAUDE.md).

4) Wire to Snipara indexing
- Implement a small service that receives persisted-change events and calls your Snipara index/update API to re-index the changed doc(s).
- Keep the index payload minimal (only changed chunks) to avoid wasted token budget.
- Update .mcp.json if new files are added (Project Setup phase).

5) LLM/context integration
- When a user or agent queries the LLM, call rlm_context_query to get the optimized context for that session/file(s). This will reflect the latest indexed content.
- Decide LITE vs FULL using the Mode Scoring checklist. For quick fixes use LITE flow; for multi-file collaborative refactors use FULL (Plan → Decompose → chunk-by-chunk).

6) Session continuity & memory
- Use rlm_remember to save important decisions (design choices, reviewer notes) during a collaborative session so future sessions can rlm_recall them.
- Consider snapshotting key checkpoints (commits or CRDT snapshots) to link to rlm_remember entries.

7) Security & permissions
- Enforce authentication (JWT/OAuth) at websocket and HTTP endpoints.
- Implement document-level ACLs in the collaboration server and block unauthorized indexing calls.
- Rate-limit re-indexing to avoid spamming Snipara during rapid edits (debounce to ~1–5s or on save).

8) UX & developer workflow
- Show presence/awareness (who’s editing which file/line).
- Offer “Save snapshot / create review” that triggers a FULL-mode plan for multi-file changes.
- Provide visual diff/conflict indicators and an explicit “Re-index to LLM” button for users who want the LLM to use the latest snapshot immediately.

9) Testing and rollout
- Start with a pilot repo, enable LITE mode for single-file collaborative edits.
- Validate: edits -> persisted state -> index event -> rlm_context_query returns updated content.
- Gradually enable FULL workflows for larger collaborative sessions.

Operational considerations
- Scalability: shard websocket servers; persist to durable storage; rate-limit indexing.
- Offline edits: support local CRDT persistence and sync when reconnects, then re-index.
- Audit and versioning: store CRDT snapshots keyed to commits; use these for reproducible rlm_context_query results.
- Monitoring: track websocket sessions, failed index attempts, and latency from edit -> indexed -> rlm_context_query.

Minimal example architecture
- Frontend: Monaco + Yjs + y-websocket client
- Collab server: y-websocket cluster (k8s) + Redis for awareness
- Persistence: service that writes Yjs updates to Postgres/S3 and debounces index calls
- Indexer: small backend that calls Snipara index/update API and updates .mcp.json if needed
- LLM flow: rlm_context_query → user LLM

If you want, I can:
- suggest specific code snippets for Monaco+Yjs connection and the debounce re-index hook,
- outline the minimal API contract between your persistence service and Snipara indexer,
- or propose a deployment diagram for k8s. Which would be most useful?
RLM stands for "Recursive Language Models" — the internal project codename for Snipara's context‑optimization engine and MCP server.
The MCP server requires Python 3.10 or newer (Python 3.10+). The Dockerfile/build uses Python 3.12.

When a user exceeds their monthly query limit the API returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests and a JSON error with rate-limit details. Example:

{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
  "usage": {
    "current": 101,
    "max": 100,
    "exceeded": true,
    "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

Key fields:
- current: usage this billing period
- max: allowed queries (‑1 = unlimited)
- exceeded: true when limit reached
- reset_at: ISO timestamp when the monthly limit resets

Rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) are also included in responses.

If the context engine finds no relevant documents, rlm_context_query does NOT fabricate content. Instead it returns an empty (or minimal) context response plus metadata and suggestions. Concretely:

What the response includes
- sections: [] (empty array) — no content sections selected
- total_tokens: 0 (or only tokens used by any included session/shared tips)
- truncated: false
- session_context: included if there is prior session context
- suggestions: an array of guidance (e.g., broaden the query, check specific docs)
- first_query_tips_included: true only on the first call in a session (you may still see the tool tips)

Example (simplified)
{
  "sections": [],
  "total_tokens": 0,
  "truncated": false,
  "session_context": null,
  "suggestions": [
    "Broaden your query (e.g. 'authentication' → 'JWT authentication flow')",
    "Try search_mode: 'semantic' or 'hybrid'",
    "Check docs/security.md"
  ],
  "first_query_tips_included": false
}

Why this happens
- The engine greedily selects only candidate sections that match the query within the token budget. If none meet relevance thresholds, nothing is returned.
- Shared/shared-best-practices content is loaded first (per MCP order). If shared context is available and relevant, you may still get shared sections even when no project-specific documents match.

Recommended next steps when you see an empty result
- Broaden or rephrase the query (remove stopwords, use synonyms, or ask a higher-level question).
- Switch search_mode to "semantic" or "hybrid" (better recall for conceptual matches).
- Increase max_tokens so more candidate content can be returned.
- Use rlm_decompose to split a complex question into smaller sub-queries.
- Check the suggestions returned (they often point to likely files to inspect).

Cache behavior
- Empty results may be cached (QueryCache). If documents are added/updated later, cache invalidation will allow fresh results.

If you want, tell me the query you used and I can suggest a rewording or try a different search_mode.

Short answer: because the OAuth Device Flow is the safest, most user-friendly, and standards-based way to authenticate CLI apps without asking users to copy long-lived API keys.

Why we chose it (key reasons):

- Security first
  - Avoids sharing or storing raw API keys on the CLI or pasting them into terminals (reduces accidental leaks and git commits).
  - Tokens are short/limited-lived (expires_in / refresh_token model) and can be revoked centrally — better rotation and incident response.
  - Works well with SSO and MFA (user authenticates in the browser), so organization-level access controls apply.

- Great CLI UX
  - Simple flow for users: run a CLI command, open a URL, enter a short code — no manual key management.
  - Works across devices and environments where the CLI can’t open a browser directly.

- Standards-based and interoperable
  - Uses RFC 8628 (OAuth Device Flow), a well-known standard — easier to reason about, test, and integrate with identity providers.
  - Supports the usual OAuth primitives (access + refresh tokens, scopes) so we can enforce least privilege.

- Operational benefits
  - Centralized authentication and auditing (who authorized what and when).
  - Easier to integrate with existing identity providers and enterprise tooling.
  - Cleaner developer experience for users and admins.

Implementation notes (how it fits our system)
- CLI initiates device flow; server returns a code and user URL. CLI polls POST /api/oauth/device/token until success or error (authorization_pending).
- Tokens use the snipara_at_ / snipara_rt_ format and are validated in the backend via DB lookup + expiry checks.
- We still provide fallbacks for dev convenience (API keys, SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH) when needed.

In short: Device Flow balances security, UX, and operational control for CLI authentication, so it’s the right fit for Snipara’s CLI/MCP scenario.

Use the project conventions — keep route handlers thin, strongly typed, validated, tested, and layered. Quick checklist you can follow when adding a new API endpoint:

1. File location
   - Create the route at apps/web/src/app/api/<resource>/route.ts (or apps/web/src/app/api/<resource>/<id>/route.ts when needed).

2. Follow the layering/architecture
   - Route → Service → Repository (lib/services/ → lib/db/queries/).
   - Route handlers must not contain business logic or direct DB calls; delegate to a service or query.

3. Route handler pattern
   - Authenticate first (requireAuth or requirePlatformAdmin for admin routes).
   - Parse and validate input with a Zod schema.
   - Delegate work to a service or db query.
   - Return standard responses and use handleError on failure.
   - Example flow: requireAuth() → schema.parse(await request.json()) → someService(...) → successResponse(...).

4. TypeScript rules
   - Project uses strict mode: explicit return types, strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny.
   - Never use any — use proper types or unknown + type guards.
   - Prefer interface for extendable object shapes.
   - Exported functions must have explicit return types.

5. Import ordering
   - 1) External packages (node_modules)
   - 2) Internal packages (@rlm-saas/*)
   - 3) Absolute imports (@/*)
   - 4) Relative imports
   - Leave blank lines between groups.

6. DB access
   - Add DB logic in lib/db/queries/ (centralized CRUD only).
   - Use prisma models/fields naming and patterns already in the repo.

7. Validation & security
   - Use Zod schemas for input validation.
   - Enforce authorization checks (project-scoped checks, API key validation where applicable).
   - For admin endpoints use requirePlatformAdmin().

8. Tests
   - Add route tests (route.test.ts) covering success, validation errors, auth failures, and edge cases.
   - Add unit tests for service logic where applicable.

9. Linting & type-checking
   - Before merge run: pnpm lint && pnpm type-check && pnpm test

10. Naming & file organization
    - Use camelCase for functions/variables, PascalCase for interfaces/classes, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants.
    - Add any new types under utils & types (lowest layer) if reusable.

11. Error handling & responses
    - Use centralized handleError / successResponse helpers.
    - Return appropriate HTTP status codes and consistent JSON shapes.

12. Extra notes
    - If the endpoint touches automation/mcp, accept both project IDs and slugs where applicable.
    - Follow existing patterns in similar endpoints (copy the thin handler + service + query pattern).

If you want, tell me the resource name and method and I can scaffold the exact route.ts, Zod schema, service signature, and test file names following these standards.

  - Agents on the TEAM plan require a Context TEAM (or ENTERPRISE) subscription.
